one_liner: A 58-year-old fair-skinned man presents with a changing dark mole on his upper back that his wife noticed has grown and darkened over the past 4 months.

stage2.hpi_extension: The lesion has been present for years as a small, uniformly tan macule, but over the past several months it has enlarged, developed irregular edges, and taken on areas of dark brown, black, and even a reddish-blue hue. It occasionally itches and bled once after being scratched. He denies pain. He is a recreational sailor and reports multiple blistering sunburns in his youth.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension. Dysplastic nevi excised twice in his 40s.
stage2.surgical_history: Two prior shave excisions of benign-appearing nevi.
stage2.family_history: Father died of 'skin cancer that spread everywhere' at age 62.
stage2.social_history: Retired construction foreman in Florida. Rarely wore sunscreen. Non-smoker; social alcohol.
stage2.physical_exam: Skin: Fitzpatrick type II, multiple solar lentigines and scattered nevi. On the left upper back is an 8 mm pigmented lesion with asymmetry, irregular and notched borders, variegated color (tan, dark brown, black, and blue-red), and a slightly raised central area. No ulceration. No palpable left axillary or cervical lymphadenopathy. Remainder of exam unremarkable.

lab_results.result: Full-thickness excisional biopsy shows atypical melanocytes with prominent nucleoli arranged in nests and as single cells at the dermoepidermal junction with pagetoid upward scatter into the epidermis. Invasion into the papillary dermis is present. Breslow depth 1.4 mm, no ulceration, mitotic rate 2/mm^2. S-100, HMB-45, and Melan-A positive.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Histology confirms invasive cutaneous melanoma. Breslow thickness is the single most important prognostic factor and determines need for sentinel lymph node biopsy and margin width.

lab_results.result: Tumor molecular profiling: BRAF V600E mutation detected. NRAS and c-KIT wild type.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A BRAF V600E mutation is present in roughly half of cutaneous melanomas and permits targeted therapy with BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib), often combined with MEK inhibitors.

lab_results.result: PET/CT for staging shows no evidence of regional or distant metastatic disease. Left axillary sentinel lymph node mapping planned.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Absence of distant disease supports localized (stage II) melanoma; sentinel lymph node biopsy will refine nodal staging.

stage5.final_clue: Wide local excision with 2 cm margins and sentinel lymph node biopsy show one micrometastasis in the left axilla. He is started on adjuvant pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitor) and counseled on routine dermatologic surveillance and sun protection.

debrief.classic_presentation: Melanoma classically occurs in fair-skinned adults with a history of intense UV exposure and presents as a new or changing pigmented lesion. The ABCDE criteria help distinguish melanoma from benign nevi: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter greater than 6 mm, and Evolution over time. Risk factors include fair skin, blistering sunburns, multiple or dysplastic nevi, personal or family history of melanoma, and immunosuppression.
debrief.pathophysiology: Melanoma arises from malignant transformation of melanocytes, most often at the dermoepidermal junction. UV radiation induces DNA damage and drives activating mutations in the MAPK pathway, most commonly BRAF V600E, as well as NRAS and c-KIT. Tumor cells show pagetoid upward scatter and eventually invade the dermis. Metastasis first occurs via lymphatics to regional nodes, followed by hematogenous spread to lung, liver, brain, and bone.
debrief.treatment_overview: Definitive treatment is wide local excision with margins based on Breslow depth. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is indicated for tumors >=0.8 mm or with high-risk features. Adjuvant therapy for node-positive or high-risk disease includes PD-1 inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) and, for BRAF-mutant disease, BRAF + MEK inhibitors (vemurafenib/dabrafenib + trametinib). CTLA-4 blockade (ipilimumab) is used for advanced disease. Prevention focuses on sun protection and full-skin surveillance.
high_yield_pearls: ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution — screen any changing pigmented lesion.
Breslow depth (tumor thickness in mm) is the single most important prognostic factor in cutaneous melanoma.
Excisional (full-thickness) biopsy — not shave biopsy — is the preferred diagnostic procedure so depth can be measured accurately.
BRAF V600E mutation is present in ~50% of cutaneous melanomas and is targeted by vemurafenib and dabrafenib.
Advanced/metastatic melanoma responds to immune checkpoint inhibitors: PD-1 (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) and CTLA-4 (ipilimumab).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Skin cancer — Melanoma' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Pigmented skin disorders' section.
